content:
# Mark Chapter 3 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), folios 32v–33r (PDF pages 64–65) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_003_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_003_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Witness:** Codex Vaticanus (B, 03) — consulted via [DigiVatLib](https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209)

---

¹ Jesus entered the synagogue again, and a man with a crippled hand was there.

² His opponents watched him like hawks, waiting to see if he would heal on the Sabbath so they could bring charges against him.

³ Jesus said to the man with the withered hand, "Stand up here in front of everyone."

⁴ Then he turned to them: "Is it lawful on the Sabbath to do good, or to do evil? To save a life, or to destroy one?" They said nothing.

⁵ He looked around at them, burning with anger, deeply grieved by the stubborn hardness of their hearts. Then he said to the man, "Stretch out your hand." He stretched it out, and his hand was completely restored.

⁶ The Pharisees walked out and immediately began plotting with the Herodians to kill Jesus.

⁷ Jesus withdrew with his disciples to the lake, and a huge crowd followed—people from Galilee, from Judea,

⁸ from Jerusalem, from Idumea, from across the Jordan, and from the region of Tyre and Sidon. This enormous crowd came because they had heard about everything he was doing.

⁹ He told his disciples to keep a boat ready for him so the crowd wouldn't crush him,

¹⁰ because he had healed so many people that everyone with a disease was pushing forward to touch him.

¹¹ Whenever evil spirits encountered him, they threw themselves down before him, screaming, "You are the Son of God!"

¹² But he gave them strict orders not to reveal who he was.

¹³ Then Jesus went up on a mountainside and called to himself the ones he wanted, and they came to him.

¹⁴ He appointed twelve—calling them apostles—to be his constant companions and to be sent out to preach,

¹⁵ with authority to drive out demons.

¹⁶ These are the twelve he appointed: Simon, whom he named Peter;

¹⁷ James son of Zebedee and his brother John—he nicknamed them Boanerges, meaning "Sons of Thunder";

¹⁸ Andrew, Philip, Bartholomew, Matthew, Thomas, James son of Alphaeus, Thaddaeus, Simon the Zealot,

¹⁹ and Judas Iscariot, who would later betray him.

²⁰ When Jesus went home, such a crowd gathered again that he and his disciples couldn't even stop to eat.

²¹ When his own family heard what was happening, they set out to take custody of him, because they were saying, "He's lost his mind."

²² Meanwhile, the religious scholars who had come down from Jerusalem were claiming, "He's possessed by Beelzebul! He drives out demons by the power of the demon-prince."

²³ So Jesus called them over and challenged them with these illustrations: "How can Satan drive out Satan?

²⁴ A kingdom torn apart by civil war will collapse.

²⁵ A household divided against itself cannot survive.

²⁶ If Satan is fighting against himself, he's finished—his end has come.

²⁷ In fact, you can't break into a strong man's house and steal his possessions unless you first overpower and tie up the strong man. Then you can ransack his house.

²⁸ I tell you the truth: every sin and every slander can be forgiven.

²⁹ But whoever slanders the Holy Spirit will never be forgiven—that person carries the guilt of an eternal sin."

³⁰ He said this because they were claiming he was empowered by an evil spirit.

³¹ Then his mother and brothers arrived. They stood outside the house and sent someone in to call him.

³² A crowd was sitting around Jesus, and they told him, "Your mother and your brothers are outside looking for you."

³³ He looked at them and said, "Who is my mother? Who are my brothers?"

³⁴ Then he looked at the people sitting around him in a circle and said, "Here they are—my mother and my brothers!

³⁵ Anyone who does what God wants is my brother, my sister, and my mother."

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

1. **"Burning with anger" (v. 5):** Mark is fearless about showing Jesus's full humanity. Jesus is furious—and simultaneously heartbroken. The Greek pairs ὀργή (anger) with a compound verb for deep co-suffering (συλλυπέομαι). This is not serene displeasure; it's a man enraged at willful cruelty disguised as piety.

2. **"Watched him like hawks" (v. 2):** The Greek παρατηρέω means to watch closely, with hostile intent. The predatory metaphor captures the adversarial surveillance.

3. **"The Zealot" (v. 18):** The Greek ΚΑΝΑΝΑΙΟΝ is actually Aramaic for "zealous one." I rendered it as "Zealot" (following Luke's translation into Greek) because the Aramaic term is opaque to modern readers while "Zealot" communicates the political dimension of Simon's identity.

4. **"He's lost his mind" (v. 21):** The Greek ἐξέστη literally means "he is out of himself"—he has gone mad. This is one of the most shocking verses in the Gospels: Jesus's own family thought he was insane. Mark does not soften this. Neither do I.

5. **"Slanders the Holy Spirit" (vv. 28-29):** I rendered βλασφημέω as "slanders" rather than the technical "blasphemes" to make the gravity visceral rather than merely theological. The sin in context is specific: attributing the work of God's Spirit to Satan (v. 30).

6. **"Anyone who does what God wants is my brother, my sister, and my mother" (v. 35):** Jesus redefines family not by blood but by obedience. Coming immediately after his own family tried to remove him by force (v. 21), this is a profound—and personally costly—declaration.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
